speaker: interviewer
text: Tape number three, September 28, 1995. Regina Gaska is the survivor. We're talking-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we were talking about when you first got to Auschwitz. They gave you a number?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was-- what's the number?

speaker: interviewer
text: And your sister's number?

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the living conditions like here in Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Auschwitz was miserable, terrible. Terrible. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You slept-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I have someplace a picture. I don't know here where it is. We slept three-- six people like in a little drawer. On the top was six, and the bottom was six. Three and three, we slept, like in a drawer, just slide in. Like the horses, the hay. They used to put in the hay for the horses. And we slept inside in there in the places. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the blankets. They didn't give us blankets, yes. And we had to wash our clothes. We washed our clothes in the-- it was like between the wires, was like running water. We washed our clothes and put it underneath at night. We slept on the wet clothes. In the morning, it was dry, and the shoes was our pillow. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] So--

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were with your sister? Any-- any cousins, still?

speaker: interviewee
text: No cousins, no. My cousins were on the other side of the-- of the wires. They stayed at family camp. They came before us. They stayed two families together. They worked in the [? bristles ?] there. And after this, they liquidate Majdanek. They liquidate them too. They sent them to the gas chambers. There was none of them survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: What work did you do at Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: At Auschwitz, I worked the shoe Kommandos.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was shoes from the transports.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what would that entail?

speaker: interviewee
text: Entailed to rip the soles apart, the soles separate, the-- this separate, the bottom separate. And inside, we found the money, sometimes, and gold in the shoes, hidden places. And we put them in separate places. And you put them together-- you've got a match, you put the shoes together. And the bottoms don't get to match, you rip them apart.

speaker: interviewer
text: If the shoes matched, then you kept them because somebody else would wear them?

speaker: interviewee
text: This-- they would sell them to the-- the Germans collected them. It was boxes. Pairs-- the pairs in-- most of the time, we ripped them apart. We didn't give them the satisfaction. We burned a lot of shoes when she didn't see it. We did a lot of sabotage. We didn't want to give them everything. We find money, we sabotaged the money too. We burned everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: You burnt the money too?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: How closely were you supervised?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not too close. It was about 200 people in this Kommando.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It was an old, nasty woman. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Was very nasty. We were-- the Jewish girl that supervised us. She was excellent. She was so good. She used to tell us, Marco is coming, We gave her a name. When she came, we started to work [INAUDIBLE]. And when she wasn't around, we'd sabotage.

speaker: interviewer
text: What other ways would you sabotage? You said you'd burn--

speaker: interviewee
text: Burn the shoes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't do the work. We find some things, we burned it. We didn't give it over to the Germans, Once I did, because I was so hungry. They gave us extra bread. It was something about $2, paper money. I didn't notice a dollar, so that's regular money. It wasn't Polish money. So we gave them. I got the extra bread from the Red Cross.

speaker: interviewer
text: From the Red Cross? Where-- where was--

speaker: interviewee
text: In Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the Red Cross come to Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: They said it's from  the Red Cross.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you never saw anybody from the Red Cross.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many hours a day were you doing the-- ripping the shoes apart from the transports?

speaker: interviewee
text: We went in the morning, and we came back about 5:00. It depends on the day. The long-- the days were. On shorter days, we came home earlier. On longer days, we came home later.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did Auschwitz look like?

speaker: interviewee
text: First was Birkenau. Birkenau was murder. When you put in your feet in the-- it's like clay. You couldn't take the shoes out. But Auschwitz, I was picked to Auschwitz. Auschwitz was beautiful. It was buildings from bricks. The floors were beautiful. We had toilets. We had signs. It was possible for the Germans. The signs are to keep yourself clean, everything. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But-- back when-- but this was by the end. When the Red Cross came, they showed us how nice they-- they're keeping the people, how clean they are, and everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did the Red Cross come?

speaker: interviewee
text: Must be by the end, a couple months by the end.

speaker: interviewee
text: '44, No. By the end of '44.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the Red Cross coming?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't see it, no. Because this is what they said, is it's the Red Cross.

speaker: interviewer
text: They said that they were coming, so they made everything clean and nice before they came.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. They stay and say Appell. And they came over, and they looked us up. And we were told, wear the [INAUDIBLE] the way they liked it, and clean, and to look presentative. To look nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anybody from the Red Cross see you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I never saw them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know anybody in the camp who saw the Red Cross? So you were just told they were coming, and that was it.

speaker: interviewee
text: They're coming, that's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Auschwitz was pretty?

speaker: interviewee
text: Auschwitz was-- was pretty. Yes, the town was pretty, yeah. But the chimneys wasn't pretty. The smoke was going 24 hours. You could smell it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did Auschwitz smell like? What did it smell like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Dead meat.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did people from the town-- did you see them at all, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: We saw people from-- from my own town.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, no, I mean people who lived nearby, did they see-- did they come--

speaker: interviewee
text: The Polish people? Yes, they saw it. They saw everything. They was laughing, making fun.

speaker: interviewer
text: You saw them laughing at you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure. And everybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they come into the camp? Where did you see them?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they weren't allowed to come into camp. They went out to the gate. When you marched to work, you saw them. They took you out of camp when you went to work. You were guarded from both sides, amid dogs and then amid the German soldiers. So you-- the Polish people passed by. They make fun of you.

speaker: interviewer
text: What sorts of things would they say?

speaker: interviewee
text: Here goes the Jews. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] It's still too many of them. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We didn't pay attention to them. They were Polish people too, in Poland. They kept them as prostitutes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who kept who as prostitutes?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Polish people. A lot of people were in there as prostitutes.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They wore short dresses, with angora sweaters. You know what's angora sweaters is? Cashmere sweaters, angora sweaters. And you recognized them, the way they are. So this is for the soldiers. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They had experiments for Jewish kids, not for them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know anybody who was experimented on?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: What happened? They gave them medicine. They gave them all kind of live experiments and with all kind of things. [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: What would happen to the-- to them after the experiments?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some of them survived and some of them died from the experiment.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it mostly children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Most of them children, young children, yeah. Twins, children. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Auschwitz was a death camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever witness-- what sorts of things did you witness? Did you witness a lot of shootings, torture? What did you see?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, shootings, we got so used to it, it didn't bother us no more. We said, next day, maybe it's me.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was no feeling at all. We were so-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we want to get over it already. It's too much, already. We couldn't take it no more. Because who cares already? She's the lucky one. She died already. She's dead already.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you think kept you going, kept you alive?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I really don't know. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And when-- when we talked together with my [INAUDIBLE], I says, what keep us alive? Why did we fight still? I really don't know. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I went-- when I was-- when we had to leave Auschwitz in 1945, my sister was taken away by the end of '44. Came out-- it came out you're not allowed to burn no more. And you never [INAUDIBLE] from me. Your sister is going to be alive at work. You know they said they're not going to burn. Maybe she's still alive, but it was too late. The men saved-- the men got saved, but the women not.

speaker: interviewer
text: Toward the end, you mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did any other incidents happen at Auschwitz while you were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Auschwitz was there. They did-- three girls sabotaged and took out ammunition, and they hanged them in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have to watch the hanging?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We stayed at night and watched the hanging.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you eat at Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: The same rations as we got, the slice of bread in the morning and in the evening, and a cup of soup.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is this where you said that the people were trying to keep track of the Jewish calendar? Do you remember any praying? Did you pray at Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, there was nothing to pray. What is there to pray? We knew it's-- next day we are gone. What's there to pray? [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Maybe next is me. I'll be free.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you aware of any underground movement at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Auschwitz, we did discover underground movement, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you discover?

speaker: interviewee
text: We discovered the-- the sabotage. The girls took out ammunition. We says the underground is there, and we're going to be liberated soon. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So that time, we knew it's-- something is moving around now, but we don't know what it's going to be. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel when you started hearing things like that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we started to talk to each other, say maybe go find a relative, maybe-- still didn't think everybody-- from six of us, I'm the only one? That's impossible. Somebody has to survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anybody try to escape?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, two girls tried to escape. They got them back in the-- a couple of days later.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to them?

speaker: interviewee
text: They put them to death. They cut their veins. They cut their veins themselves.

speaker: interviewer
text: The girls?

speaker: interviewee
text: They escaped, and they bring them back. They knew what's waiting for them, so they cut their veins. Somebody gave them razor blades. They cut their veins to death.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they make-- did they punish you when they escaped?

speaker: interviewee
text: We stayed [INAUDIBLE] Appell longer. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They count them like diamonds, that they shouldn't-- nobody should miss. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It's always punishment, [INAUDIBLE] Appell, at the walls stand, anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- did people-- did you see people killing themselves just to get it over with?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, by the wires. They went to the-- to the gates-- electric gates, so-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] [INAUDIBLE] I went a couple of times. I wanted to see my uncle. Maybe, maybe, maybe, maybe, maybe, maybe. There's nothing there. You looked at like-- you're looking around at tiny little things. Maybe you'll find somebody that recognizes you or that you recognize them. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I didn't recognize my sister when they cut her hair off. She didn't recognize me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there any other work that you did other than the shoes at Auschwitz, other than ripping the shoes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Shoes, we did. No, most of them, we did the shoes, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened when your sister was taken? When-- that was the end of '44 she was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was a selection?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. She stayed-- it was very cold that time. It was freezing. And she didn't feel good a couple of months before. And she says, I don't feel like going out to work. She says, maybe I'll skip today. They all should know it. I says, work-- let's go. We're both going to go and [INAUDIBLE]. She-- you go, I'll stay. And there was a selection. And they was showing the tongue.

speaker: interviewer
text: There was what?

speaker: interviewee
text: The tongue.

speaker: interviewer
text: The tongue?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was what they said to do? Yeah?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, they're supposed to show the tongue, and they didn't like it. They put her name and the number down, and that's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was gassed in Auschwitz, taken away from me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you came back from work and you found out?

speaker: interviewee
text: I found out that her name is on the-- they took her name down. We cried. The next day, they took her away from me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you knew that-- the two of you were together, and you knew that she'd be going the next day?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And I said, I want to go too, to the Kapo. He slapped me. She says, another day, you're going to go. Why are you rushing? You're going to go next.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the last thing you and your sister said to each other?

speaker: interviewee
text: She wanted to give me her bread. I says, no, please, eat your bread. She gave me a sweater, and she says, I don't need it no more. [SOBBING] And we cried. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And she says to me, maybe you tell the story.

speaker: interviewer
text: How much longer were you at Auschwitz for?

speaker: interviewee
text: Till the liberation.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, weren't you taken to-- in January to another camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we traveled, yes. After the-- when we left Auschwitz, they took us the-- to Ravensbruck. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We stayed there about two weeks. I don't know the dates, how long we stayed there. We didn't do any work there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know that the war was coming to an end?

speaker: interviewee
text: We knew something is moving. However, we didn't know exactly, because we are always with the Germans. So we didn't know what's really happening.

speaker: interviewer
text: How far was Ravensbruck from Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I have no idea.

speaker: interviewer
text: You don't remember how you got there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think we went by train this time, to Ravensbruck. Ravensbruck, I remember the walls were so tall, bricks. We couldn't see the sky. It was so crowded. And when you didn't know how to shovel [INAUDIBLE], you couldn't get a little soup, nothing. You dropped dead from [INAUDIBLE]. Ravensbruck is a murder, plain murder. The fountain was outside, was [INAUDIBLE], and there you washed. No matter how cold it was, you washed in the fountains. This I remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: What sort of work did you do there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. We didn't do any work, no. That's why it was so bad, because we knew we're going to our death. No work, so we figure, we're not going to make it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you think kept you going through this?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I really don't know. I really don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you with anybody you knew here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, not from my own town. We knew people what survived, and they're now in Israel, where a group, five of us, kept together. One is in Milwaukee. A few are in Israel. Most of them are in Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this camp near, do you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then they took us from this camp to Malchow Camp. In Malchow, we didn't do anything, neither we slept on the floor all the time. It was not bad. It's nothing. People were dying, buried in front of the-- what do you call it-- in front of the gate. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then they took us in another camp someplace. We slept overnight on the street. I saw the German women doing the laundry, or something there. That's what I remember. I don't know where it was, what camp it was. Then they took us to Leipzig.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was basically a death march.

speaker: interviewee
text: Death march, yeah. This is the death march all the way, every time a different camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was from January to April?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, April 26, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So on this death march, how man-- did you move almost every night? Did you keep moving?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not a couple-- a week, or two weeks. We moved all the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about this death march? Do you know how many girls were on it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, our group was about 10. We survived. The rest didn't survive. The group was a long group. But every-- every other day, either somebody fell asleep, or they died, or they killed, or something.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they were guarding you very heavily?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. In Leipzig, by the march-- I don't know was-- we were laying, like, in a deep hole they put us down. I figure they're going to shoot us, that this is proper way. It's like in a forest.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel when they put--

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't care anything no more We were so ready to go. We were-- we didn't care what's going to happen. What's going to happen is going to happen. We were about six-- six or eight girls already left. We were laying in the big-- oh, and we looked up on the top. The Germans were marching. Oh, and we saw burning them something there, like a fire. So one of us was a girl much older than us. She says, something is wrong. They're burning papers. Maybe it's the end. And this was the end.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did-- on-- during-- while the death march was happening, did anybody run away?

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't have to run. They'd shoot them like flies.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did-- did anybody try to get away?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure. They tried to get and they-- and they-- to grab a piece of grass to eat, or the-- a piece of [INAUDIBLE] or a potato.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they would kill them for that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, would kill them for that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you try to get away?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I didn't. What's going to happen happen. I don't care no more. You walked. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I couldn't walk no more, and my friend says, you have to. You have to. You have to. And this is the way it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you kept each other alive?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in Leipzig for?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't stay too long in Leipzig.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened after they put you in the hole, and you go thought they were going to shoot you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we marched out. We marched out by ourselves, because we saw no Germans no more. So we figured we might as well come out from the hole and see what it's all about.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many girls were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were about eight already left. One was sleeping on top of the other because there was snow. We kept warm, exchanging. One slept this-- at this time  of the hour. And then the-- and we-- and we said to ourselves, this must be the end. And we saw a German carrying bread, so one of the girls grabbed the-- the bread from the German woman. And she started to scream. Some German says, you don't have to grab the  bread. You're going to be free soon. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So we came out and we took a train. And we came to Leipzig something. We stayed in Leipzig. They took us to Leipzig. I don't remember. Wait, how did you get on this train?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were trains running.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were-- you were just along with the gir-- with your friends right now.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We're all with my friends, girls.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you feeling? What did you think was going on?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they-- they took us with a train or something. It's very cloudy in my head from this. In Leipzig, we didn't do anything. Here, they did take us to clean [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] movie houses or something in Leipzig.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who-- who liberated you?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- the liberators-- one told us, this way is the Russian peop-- soldiers, and this way is the American. So we choose to go on the American side. We liberated us ourselves. But because we were a small group already. Who knew about us? Nobody.

speaker: interviewer
text: So basically, you were free from the time that you were in that hole. And then you came out, and there was nobody there anymore.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know what day that was?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who else did you come in contact with? I mean, this German woman who said, you don't have to give me-- I don't have-- you don't need my bread. You're going to be free. How did you feel when she said that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't believe her. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There was a woman, a much older woman. She must be now in the 9-- when she's still alive in Israel, she must be in the 90s. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] She was very-- what do you say? She was very alert. She knew how to-- to deal with this thing, grab things, and making and cooking.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was she grabbing and cooking?

speaker: interviewee
text: After the war?

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, no, we're still--

speaker: interviewee
text: In the march, in the death march, when she found a chicken, she cut the chicken.

speaker: interviewer
text: During the death march?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not in the death march. After the liberation. Death march, you couldn't do anything. You ate all what's in the grass. You ate grass, but it didn't belong. People got diarrhea. They made-- excuse me-- and-- wherever they were sitting, they were making from the grass, eating in the street to survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: There was no water. There was no sanitary, nothing. You couldn't wash in the march. How could you do anything in the march? So when you got out of that hole and there was nobody there, and you saw this woman--

speaker: interviewee
text: We start marching ourselves.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were just walking yourself.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you went on a train?

speaker: interviewee
text: We went on a train. We--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have to pay? Did you have to pay? No? What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: We just went on the train.

speaker: interviewer
text: You're just going on the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: We wound up in Leipzig. Before the camp we left, we wound up there. And we came to the American side, so they crossed us a bridge. The Americans took us on their [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you-- how did you feel when you saw Americans?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't feel anything. We fe-- felt numb. We didn't know anything. We felt so numb. They crossed us the bridge. They led us. We had to go down all the way, and they picked us up on the other side, something. But the bridges was exploded. They were-- you couldn't cross a bridge there. So we wound up in Leipzig, in a school.

speaker: interviewer
text: They put you in a school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember anything they said to you, the Americans?

speaker: interviewee
text: The American said, you're free. That's all. What does it mean, free? It wasn't. We didn't know what to do now, because we were lost, like this, where somebody to care about us, go this way, go this way. After the liberation, we didn't know what to do. We were lost. They sent us to the [INAUDIBLE] school. We stayed there. And everyone in the school got sick. Oh, sick, [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Ate too much?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, from the grass.

speaker: interviewer
text: From the grass, still.

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody got diarrhea. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then they-- they served us-- the German cooked, and they served us lunches or something there.

speaker: interviewer
text: The German cooked now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this a prisoner of the Americans now who was cooking?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. The Americans arranged that the German should cook for the prisoners, the survivors. In-- on the road, too, they said the German offered bread for the survivors to march. They wasn't allowed to kill them no more. They gave an order you have to stop killing, feed them with bread.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, that was during the march, at the end?

speaker: interviewee
text: By the end, yeah. They gave us bread. The bread, you could-- like clay. You couldn't eat them. You couldn't swallow them. You'd throw it in the garbage. The only thing we did was drink a lot of water. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we got sick in Leipzig. Oh, we got sick We couldn't eat.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was in the school, once you--

speaker: interviewee
text: In the school. We couldn't eat. We couldn't drink. We were laying like lambs. The toilet was flooded. They made us clean the toilet.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Americans made you clean the toilet?

speaker: interviewee
text: We couldn't do it, and we had to do it. They made us do everything. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then a rabbi came and told us how we should behave. He told us we should-- the American are very nice. When you don't-- you say no to them, they wouldn't bother you. He said kaddish. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] He said a prayer. And we stayed in Leipzig not long. And they told us again, this way to go or this way to go. We took the American side to go.
